title: Post-Disaster Reforestation and Restoration Act
plain_summary: This bill creates a program run by the Department of the Interior to help replant trees and fix damaged land after disasters like wildfires and storms. The program will focus on federal lands and Indian lands that cannot recover naturally without help. Each year, the department will make a list of the most important areas that need to be fixed first. They can give out grants and contracts to organizations to do the work and make sure there are enough seeds and baby trees available for replanting.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Post-Disaster Reforestation and Restoration Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Post-Disaster Reforestation and Restoration Program
sections.text:
The Secretary of the Interior must create and run a program to help forests and lands recover after disasters. Within one year of this law passing, and every year after that, the Secretary must work with other government agencies to find lands that need help growing back trees and plants after events like wildfires, insect attacks, or severe weather. These are lands that will not naturally grow back without help.

The Secretary must make a list each year of the most important projects to replant trees and restore damaged land. The Secretary can pay for these projects by giving grants to organizations, making contracts with companies, making agreements with Native American tribes under existing laws, or working with partners through cooperative agreements. The Secretary can also help make sure there are enough seeds and young trees available for these projects.

Government agencies must reach out to many groups to help with this work. These groups include Native American tribes, states, territories, local governments, Alaska Native organizations, Native Hawaiian organizations, colleges and universities, other federal agencies that manage nearby land, and other interested parties.

Starting two years after this law passes, and every year after that, the Secretary must send a report to Congress. This report must include a count of all lands that need help, a list of priority projects and how they are going, information about grants and contracts given out, details about outreach efforts, and recommendations about what is needed to fix any backlog of restoration work.

This program will end seven years after this law is passed.

This section defines important terms used in the law. Covered agencies include the National Park Service, United States Fish and Wildlife Service, Bureau of Land Management, Bureau of Reclamation, and Bureau of Indian Affairs. Covered lands means federal lands managed by these agencies or Native American forest and rangeland held in trust by the United States. Indian forest land or rangeland means land held in trust for Native American tribes that has or once had forest or grass cover. Indian tribe means any federally recognized Native American or Alaska Native group. Natural regeneration means when forests grow back naturally without human help. Reforestation means replanting trees in areas where forests once grew. Restoration means helping damaged ecosystems recover by bringing back appropriate plants and natural community structure. Secretary means the Secretary of the Interior. Unplanned disturbance means unexpected events that damage ecosystems like wildfires, insect infestations, disease outbreaks, or severe weather.
